content:
# 1 Thessalonians Chapter 5 — The SOPHIA Bible (Consensus)
**Arbiter:** SOPHIA | **In council with:** EMET, LOGOS, PNEUMA
**Source Manuscript:** Codex Sinaiticus, 4th century CE | **Source Image:** source-texts/greek-NT/1TH_005_page_001.jpg

---

**5:1** Now concerning the times and the seasons, brothers, you have no need for anything to be written to you,

**5:2** for you yourselves know accurately that the day of the Lord comes as a thief in the night.

**5:3** When they say, "Peace and security," then sudden destruction comes upon them, as birth pains upon a woman with child, and they shall by no means escape.

**5:4** But you, brothers, are not in darkness, that the day should overtake you as a thief;

**5:5** for you are all sons of light and sons of the day. We are not of the night nor of darkness.

**5:6** So then, let us not sleep as the rest do, but let us watch and be sober.

**5:7** For those who sleep, sleep at night, and those who are drunk, are drunk at night.

**5:8** But we, being of the day, let us be sober, having put on the breastplate of faith and love, and as a helmet the hope of salvation.

**5:9** For God did not appoint us to wrath, but to the obtaining of salvation through our Lord Jesus Christ,

**5:10** who died for us, so that whether we watch or we sleep, we should live together with him.

**5:11** Therefore encourage one another and build up one another, just as you are also doing.

**5:12** Now we ask you, brothers, to recognize those who labor among you and who are over you in the Lord and who admonish you,

**5:13** and to esteem them exceedingly in love because of their work. Be at peace among yourselves.

**5:14** And we exhort you, brothers: admonish the disorderly, encourage the fainthearted, hold fast to the weak, be patient toward all.

**5:15** See that no one renders evil for evil to anyone, but always pursue what is good, both toward one another and toward all.

**5:16** Rejoice always.

**5:17** Pray without ceasing.

**5:18** In everything give thanks; for this is the will of God in Christ Jesus for you.

**5:19** Do not quench the Spirit.

**5:20** Do not despise prophecies.

**5:21** But test all things; hold fast what is good.

**5:22** Abstain from every form of evil.

**5:23** Now may the God of peace himself sanctify you wholly, and may your whole being — spirit and soul and body — be kept blameless at the coming of our Lord Jesus Christ.

**5:24** Faithful is he who calls you, who also will do it.

**5:25** Brothers, pray also for us.

**5:26** Greet all the brothers with a holy kiss.

**5:27** I adjure you by the Lord that this letter be read to all the brothers.

**5:28** The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you.

---

## SOPHIA's Council Notes

This chapter divides into two movements: the eschatological teaching on the Day of the Lord (vv. 1-11) and the rapid-fire community instructions (vv. 12-28). The manuscript image confirms the text spans columns 2 through 4 of the Sinaiticus folio, with clear scriptio continua and nomina sacra throughout.

**On the Day of the Lord passage (vv. 1-11):** The three translators largely agree on the substance of this section. The primary divergences were stylistic — PNEUMA's dynamic renderings ("crash down," "catch you off guard," "children of the light") versus the more formal language of EMET and LOGOS. The consensus favors the formal register here, as the apocalyptic imagery benefits from measured gravity. EMET's hyper-literal constructions (e.g., "the-[ones]-sleeping," "stands-upon") were smoothed for readability while preserving the underlying Greek structure.

**On verse 9 — "appoint" vs. "destine":** PNEUMA chose "destine" to avoid predestinarian overtones, but "appoint" (EMET, LOGOS) more faithfully renders ETHETO without introducing the very theological freight PNEUMA sought to avoid. "Appoint" was retained.

**On verse 14 — "disorderly" vs. "idle":** PNEUMA rendered ATAKTOUS as "idle," interpreting the word through its Thessalonian context (those who stopped working). EMET and LOGOS preserved the literal "disorderly," which retains the fuller semantic range of this military term. The consensus follows the literal rendering, as "idle" is an interpretive narrowing.

**On the staccato exhortations (vv. 16-22):** All three translators recognized the rapid-fire quality of these commands. The consensus preserves the brevity and directness. EMET's inverted word order ("The Spirit do-not-quench") was normalized to standard English while keeping the imperative force.

**On verse 23 — tripartite formula:** All three translators agreed that "spirit and soul and body" is comprehensive language for the whole person, not a technical trichotomy. The consensus adds "your whole being" (following PNEUMA and EMET's "your whole [being]") as a clarifying frame for the tripartite list, while maintaining the formal structure of the prayer.

**On verse 27 — the adjuration:** The intensity of ENORKIZO is notable. All three translators recognized its force. "I adjure you by the Lord" (EMET, LOGOS) was preferred over PNEUMA's "I put you under oath before the Lord" as more concise while equally solemn.

— SOPHIA, AI Translator, in council with EMET, LOGOS, and PNEUMA
